content:
# Genesis Chapter 40 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus of EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 23r–23v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_040_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_040_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 38–39

---

*The Two Officials Imprisoned — Joseph Assigned to Them (vv. 1–4)*

**40:1** And it came to pass after these things that the cupbearer of the king of Egypt and the baker offended their master, the king of Egypt. **40:2** And Pharaoh was furious with his two officers — the chief of the cupbearers and the chief of the bakers. **40:3** And he put them under guard in the house of the captain of the guard, in the prison-house — the place where Joseph was bound. **40:4** And the captain of the guard assigned Joseph to them, and he attended them. And they were under guard for some days.

---

*The Dreams of a Single Night — Joseph's Theological Question (vv. 5–8)*

**40:5** And the two of them dreamed a dream, each man his own dream, in one night — each man according to the interpretation of his dream — the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt who were bound in the prison-house.

**40:6** And Joseph came to them in the morning and saw them, and behold, they were troubled. **40:7** And he asked the officers of Pharaoh who were with him under guard in the house of his master, saying, "Why are your faces so troubled today?"

**40:8** And they said to him, "We have dreamed a dream, and there is no one to interpret it." And Joseph said to them, "Do not interpretations belong to God? Tell it to me, please."

---

*The Cupbearer's Dream — Joseph's Interpretation (vv. 9–13)*

**40:9** And the chief of the cupbearers told his dream to Joseph and said to him, "In my dream — behold, a vine was before me. **40:10** And on the vine were three branches, and it was as though it was budding — its blossom came up, and its clusters ripened into grapes. **40:11** And the cup of Pharaoh was in my hand, and I took the grapes and pressed them into the cup of Pharaoh, and I placed the cup upon the palm of Pharaoh."

**40:12** And Joseph said to him, "This is its interpretation: the three branches are three days. **40:13** In yet three days Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore you to your place, and you will place the cup of Pharaoh in his hand, according to the former custom when you were his cupbearer.

---

*Joseph's Plea (vv. 14–15)*

**40:14** "But remember me when it goes well with you, and show me, please, covenant-faithfulness — mention me to Pharaoh and bring me out of this house. **40:15** For I was indeed stolen from the land of the Hebrews, and here also I have done nothing that they should put me in the pit."

---

*The Baker's Dream — Joseph's Interpretation (vv. 16–19)*

**40:16** And the chief of the bakers saw that he had interpreted well, and he said to Joseph, "I also — in my dream, behold, three baskets of white bread were upon my head. **40:17** And in the uppermost basket were all kinds of food for Pharaoh, the work of a baker, and the birds were eating them from the basket upon my head."

**40:18** And Joseph answered and said, "This is its interpretation: the three baskets are three days. **40:19** In yet three days Pharaoh will lift your head from upon you, and he will hang you on a tree, and the birds will eat your flesh from upon you."

---

*Pharaoh's Birthday — Both Interpretations Fulfilled (vv. 20–22)*

**40:20** And it came to pass on the third day — it was the birthday of Pharaoh — and he made a feast for all his servants. And he lifted up the head of the chief of the cupbearers and the head of the chief of the bakers among his servants. **40:21** And he restored the chief of the cupbearers to his cupbearing, and he placed the cup upon the palm of Pharaoh. **40:22** And the chief of the bakers he hanged — just as Joseph had interpreted to them.

---

*The Forgetting (v. 23)*

**40:23** But the chief of the cupbearers did not remember Joseph — and he forgot him.

---

## SOPHIA Consensus Notes

### Rulings and Rationale

**1. "Offended" — chateu (v. 1): EMET upheld**

EMET renders chateu as "offended"; LOGOS renders "sinned against"; PNEUMA renders "gave offense to." The root chata' means "to sin, to miss the mark," and in 39:9 Joseph used it in its full theological weight ("sin against God"). Here the same root appears in a court context — the officials "sinned against" / "offended" their master. EMET's "offended" captures the courtly register (an offense against a king's dignity) while preserving the verbal echo. "Sinned against" (LOGOS) is more theologically loaded than the context warrants — this is a court offense, not a theological transgression. SOPHIA follows EMET: "offended."

**2. "Troubled" — zo'afim (v. 6): EMET upheld**

EMET renders zo'afim as "troubled"; LOGOS and PNEUMA render "downcast." The Hebrew zo'afim (from za'af, "to be vexed, angry, troubled") describes emotional disturbance — not merely sadness but agitation and distress. "Troubled" captures the broader range; "downcast" implies only sadness. SOPHIA follows EMET: "troubled."

**3. "Baskets of white bread" — sallei chori (v. 16): EMET/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

EMET and PNEUMA render sallei chori as "baskets of white bread." LOGOS renders "wicker baskets." The Hebrew chori is debated: it may derive from chur ("white") — giving "white bread baskets" — or from a root meaning "perforated/woven" — giving "wicker baskets." Both readings are linguistically possible. However, the LXX reads κανᾶ χονδριτῶν ("baskets of coarse bread/meal"), supporting the bread content reading. SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA: "baskets of white bread."

**4. "Pit" — bor (v. 15): All agree (3-0)**

All three translators render bor as "pit" — preserving the crucial verbal link to 37:20-24 (the pit the brothers threw Joseph into). The same word bor describes both Joseph's first imprisonment (the dry cistern in the wilderness) and his current imprisonment (the Egyptian dungeon). The thread runs from 37:24 through 40:15 to 41:14.

**5. "Prison-house" — beit haSohar (vv. 3, 5): EMET upheld (per Gen 39 precedent)**

Per the Gen 39 ruling, SOPHIA continues with "prison-house" for beit haSohar, overriding LOGOS's "house of confinement" and PNEUMA's "prison."

**6. "Covenant-faithfulness" — chesed (v. 14): All agree (3-0)**

All three translators use the project convention "covenant-faithfulness" for chesed. Joseph's plea to the cupbearer uses the same word that describes what the LORD extended to Joseph in prison (39:21) — the irony is that Joseph asks the cupbearer for the same quality of loyalty God has shown him, and the cupbearer fails entirely.

**7. "I was indeed stolen" — gunnov gunnabti (v. 15): All agree (3-0)**

All three translators preserve the infinitive absolute emphasis. SOPHIA renders "I was indeed stolen" — the emphatic English marker "indeed" corresponds to the Hebrew's intensifying infinitive absolute construction.

---

### LXX Variants Summary (Vaticanus, Pages 38–39)

| Verse | MT Reading | LXX (Vaticanus) | SOPHIA Decision |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 40:1 | חָטְאוּ (chateu — "offended/sinned") | ἥμαρτον ("sinned") | Follow MT: "offended" — courtly register preserved |
| 40:8 | הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים ("Do not interpretations belong to God?") | οὐχὶ διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ἡ διασάφησις ("Is not the interpretation through God?") | Follow MT: "belong to" (possessive) rather than "through" (agency) — MT emphasizes divine ownership of interpretation |
| 40:13 | יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשֶׁךָ ("Pharaoh will lift up your head") | λήψεται ("will take/receive your head") | Follow MT: "lift up your head" — preserving the double-meaning idiom |
| 40:15 | גֻנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי ("I was indeed stolen") | κλοπῇ ἐκλάπην ("by theft I was stolen") | Both preserve the infinitive absolute — MT followed |
| 40:15 | בַּבּוֹר ("in the pit") | εἰς τὸν λάκκον ("into the cistern/pit") | Follow MT: "pit" — LXX confirms the same term used in 37:24 |
| 40:16 | סַלֵּי חֹרִי ("baskets of white bread") | κανᾶ χονδριτῶν ("baskets of coarse bread") | Follow MT: "baskets of white bread" — LXX supports bread-content reading |
| 40:19 | יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשְׁךָ מֵעָלֶיךָ ("lift your head from upon you") | ἀφελεῖ ("will remove your head from you") | Follow MT: same idiom with devastating addition — LXX uses a different verb, dissolving the parallelism |

---

— SOPHIA, Consensus Translator | Project AI Bible
